digital_id.note: The national ID card (Personalausweis) is legally mandatory for citizens aged 16+, and as of 2026 all newly issued cards ship with the eID online-authentication function pre-activated by default (previously opt-in), enabling contract signing and bank-account opening via the AusweisApp. The eID uses a citizen-held cryptographic chip (two key pairs, a 6-digit PIN) rather than a centralized government database query at time of use — a decentralized, citizen-controlled design — but active adoption of the online eID for everyday services still lags behind Estonia's, and by end of 2026 Germany must additionally roll out the EU-wide EUDI wallet.

sources.title: Important Information on the new electronic German ID Card | Federal Foreign Office

cash_payment.note: Cash remains legal tender with no current general German cap on cash payments. A new EU-wide anti-money-laundering regulation (EU 2024/1624) will impose a €10,000 cap on cash payments accepted by businesses (private person-to-person deals excluded) starting 10 July 2027 — a future restriction, not yet in force as of mid-2026.

sources.title: EU to impose €10,000 cash payment limit from July 2027 under new AML regulation

mandatory_schooling.note: Germany enforces compulsory school attendance (Schulpflicht, not merely compulsory education) and does not permit homeschooling for pedagogical or religious reasons — only rare, narrow medical exceptions apply. This traces to the 1938 Reichsschulpflichtgesetz and has been repeatedly upheld by German courts and the European Court of Human Rights; violations can escalate from fines to short custody removals.

social_credit.note: No government social-credit system exists. SCHUFA, Germany's dominant credit bureau, is a private company (majority-owned by banks and retailers) that scores financial payment reliability only, not a state-run trustworthiness score; since March 17, 2026 it uses a unified 100–999 point scale.

sources.title: The 2026 SCHUFA score reforms and implications for financial services firms | PwC Legal

alt_currency.note: Individuals may freely buy, hold, and self-custody cryptocurrency; the government has stated it does not intend to ban crypto ownership. Crypto-asset service providers require BaFin authorization under the EU's MiCA regulation (implemented via the Kryptomärkteaufsichtsgesetz, KMAG) as of December 30, 2024. No restrictions were found on private gold ownership or trade.

sources.title: New regulatory regime for crypto assets in Germany | Norton Rose Fulbright

mandatory_insurance.note: Health insurance has been compulsory for the entire resident population since 2009, split between statutory insurance (GKV, ~90% of residents, mandatory for employees earning under roughly €64,350/year) and private insurance (PKV) for those above that threshold or self-employed; under 0.1% of the population is uninsured according to the Federal Statistical Office (Destatis).

travel_restrictions.note: General freedom of movement is respected for residents domestically and across Schengen, though Germany is among 11 Schengen states that reintroduced internal border controls in 2026 citing security and migration pressure. Separately, a CEAS Adjustment Act (GEAS-Anpassungsgesetz) passed by the Bundestag on 27 February 2026 (in force from June 2026) allows authorities to bar asylum seekers and refugees from leaving reception and 'secondary migration' centers for extended periods based on a presumption of flight risk — a restriction on a specific group, not the general population.

sources.title: Freedom of movement | Asylum Information Database (AIDA), European Council on Refugees and Exiles
Germany implements Common European Asylum System | Federal Government (Bundesregierung)

internet_restrictions.note: There is no general blocking of the open internet, but the Network Enforcement Act (NetzDG, 2018) requires platforms with 2M+ registered users to investigate and remove flagged illegal content quickly under threat of large fines, and since March 2021 an ISP-industry body (CUII) has DNS-blocked 'structurally copyright-infringing' websites. Freedom House rated Germany among the freer countries for internet access (8th of 70 rated countries in its 2022 Freedom on the Net report) while flagging controversial over-removals and rising self-censorship concerns.

sources.title: Germany: Freedom on the Net 2024 Country Report | Freedom House

free_speech.note: Article 5 of the Basic Law (Grundgesetz) guarantees freedom of expression, but it is legally bounded by hate-speech law (Volksverhetzung, §130 StGB, covering incitement against segments of the population) and by §188 StGB, which criminalizes defamation/malicious statements against politicians — investigations under §188 rose from about 1,400 in 2022 to 4,792 in 2025, prompting 2026 reform proposals. RSF also flags rising political polarization, disinformation, and pressure on journalists as growing threats to press freedom.

sources.title: Press freedom under pressure in Germany due to disinformation, political polarisation and attacks on journalism | RSF
Freedom of expression in Germany: provocation is permitted, hate speech is not | Tatsachen über Deutschland (German Federal Government)
